tasks.title: Warm-up: Create a ConfigMap and Inspect Its Contents
tasks.goal: Create a ConfigMap using two different methods (imperative / declarative YAML) and read the stored data back with kubectl.
tasks.prompt:
Create a ConfigMap using each of the following two methods.

Method A — Imperative: Use --from-literal to create app-config-cmd with two values: LOG_LEVEL=info and APP_REGION=ap-northeast-2.
Method B — Declarative: Write a YAML manifest (app-config.yaml) containing the same two values and use kubectl apply to create app-config.

After creating both, use kubectl describe to verify the Data section of each ConfigMap.
tasks.starter:
# Method A: Imperative (fill in the blanks and run)
kubectl create configmap app-config-cmd \
  --from-literal=__KEY1__=__VALUE1__ \
  --from-literal=__KEY2__=__VALUE2__

# Method B: Declarative (complete app-config.yaml)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  __KEY1__: "__VALUE1__"
  __KEY2__: "__VALUE2__"
tasks.solution:
# Method A
kubectl create configmap app-config-cmd \
  --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=info \
  --from-literal=APP_REGION=ap-northeast-2

# Method B: app-config.yaml
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  LOG_LEVEL: "info"
  APP_REGION: "ap-northeast-2"

# Apply
kubectl apply -f app-config.yaml

# Verify
kubectl describe configmap app-config-cmd
kubectl describe configmap app-config
tasks.explanation: kubectl create configmap --from-literal is quick and convenient, but leaves no audit trail. Declaring a YAML manifest enables version control in Git, and kubectl apply is the standard declarative approach — it patches existing resources or creates them if they do not yet exist. The key goal of this step is to confirm that the Data sections of both ConfigMaps are identical.
hints: The --from-literal flag takes the form KEY=VALUE. If the value contains spaces, wrap it in quotes.
Each line under the data field in YAML follows the format 'key: "value"'. Incorrect indentation (2-space indent required) will cause a parse error.
In the output of kubectl describe configmap <name>, the Data section must show both keys and their values. If a value is missing, re-check the --from-literal argument or the YAML value.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 1: Inject an Entire ConfigMap as Environment Variables with envFrom
tasks.goal: Use envFrom to inject all keys from a ConfigMap as environment variables into a Pod container, then confirm the values were delivered correctly using printenv.
tasks.prompt:
Write a Pod manifest (envfrom-pod.yaml) that uses envFrom to load the app-config ConfigMap created in Step 1.
- Image: busybox:1.36
- Container command: sh -c 'printenv | grep -E "LOG_LEVEL|APP_REGION"; sleep 3600'
- Inject app-config via envFrom.

After creating the Pod, use kubectl logs to verify the output.
tasks.starter:
# envfrom-pod.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: envfrom-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ["sh", "-c", "printenv | grep -E 'LOG_LEVEL|APP_REGION'; sleep 3600"]
      envFrom:
        - ____Ref:
            name: ____
tasks.solution:
# envfrom-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: envfrom-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ["sh", "-c", "printenv | grep -E 'LOG_LEVEL|APP_REGION'; sleep 3600"]
      envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: app-config

# Apply and verify
kubectl apply -f envfrom-pod.yaml
kubectl get pod envfrom-pod   # wait for Running status
kubectl logs envfrom-pod
tasks.explanation: envFrom.configMapRef uses every key in the ConfigMap as an environment variable name directly. If the logs show LOG_LEVEL=info and APP_REGION=ap-northeast-2, the injection was successful. The envFrom approach is convenient because you don't need to list each key individually, but be aware that all keys are dumped into the container's namespace, which can cause unintended variable name collisions.
hints: Under envFrom, the entry is either configMapRef: (for a ConfigMap) or secretRef: (for a Secret). A typo here will leave the Pod in Pending or Error state.
If kubectl logs envfrom-pod produces no output, check the Events section of kubectl describe pod envfrom-pod for error messages like configmap 'app-config' not found.
If the Pod is stuck in ContainerCreating, verify that the ConfigMap exists in the same namespace by running kubectl get configmap.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 2: Create a Secret and Inject Specific Keys with valueFrom
tasks.goal: Store database credentials in a Secret, then use valueFrom to selectively inject specific keys from both the ConfigMap and the Secret into a container under custom environment variable names. Confirm that all three values are delivered correctly.
tasks.prompt:
1. Create a Secret named db-secret using --from-literal. Include the keys: DB_USER=admin and DB_PASSWORD=s3cr3t!
2. Write a Pod manifest (valuefrom-pod.yaml) using the valueFrom method.
   - Image: busybox:1.36
   - Container command: sh -c 'echo LEVEL=$LOG_LEVEL USER=$DB_USER PW=$DB_PASSWORD; sleep 3600'
   - LOG_LEVEL comes from the app-config ConfigMap; DB_USER and DB_PASSWORD come from the db-secret Secret.
3. After creating the Pod, use kubectl logs to confirm all three values appear in the output.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Create the Secret (fill in the blanks)
kubectl create secret generic db-secret \
  --from-literal=____=____ \
  --from-literal=____=____

# 2. valuefrom-pod.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: valuefrom-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ["sh", "-c", "echo LEVEL=$LOG_LEVEL USER=$DB_USER PW=$DB_PASSWORD; sleep 3600"]
      env:
        - name: LOG_LEVEL
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: ____
              key: ____
        - name: DB_USER
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: ____
              key: ____
        - name: DB_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            ____:
              name: ____
              key: ____
tasks.solution:
# 1. Create the Secret
kubectl create secret generic db-secret \
  --from-literal=DB_USER=admin \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD='s3cr3t!'

# 2. valuefrom-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: valuefrom-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ["sh", "-c", "echo LEVEL=$LOG_LEVEL USER=$DB_USER PW=$DB_PASSWORD; sleep 3600"]
      env:
        - name: LOG_LEVEL
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: app-config
              key: LOG_LEVEL
        - name: DB_USER
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-secret
              key: DB_USER
        - name: DB_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-secret
              key: DB_PASSWORD

# Apply and verify
kubectl apply -f valuefrom-pod.yaml
kubectl logs valuefrom-pod
# Expected output: LEVEL=info USER=admin PW=s3cr3t!
tasks.explanation: The valueFrom approach lets you pull specific keys from a ConfigMap or Secret and assign them any environment variable name you choose inside the container. Use configMapKeyRef for ConfigMaps and secretKeyRef for Secrets. If you inspect the Secret with kubectl get secret db-secret -o yaml, you will see the values are base64-encoded. However, Kubernetes automatically decodes them before injecting them into the container, so application code can use the values as-is without any decoding logic.
hints: configMapKeyRef and secretKeyRef are different spellings — double-check that the prefix is 'configMapKey' or 'secretKey' before 'Ref'.
Special characters such as !, #, $, and & in Secret values can be interpreted by the shell. When using --from-literal, wrap the entire value in single quotes: --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD='s3cr3t!'
If PW= is empty in the kubectl logs output, verify that the key name in the Pod manifest exactly matches the key stored in the Secret by running kubectl describe secret db-secret.

tasks.title: Advanced: Mount a ConfigMap as a Volume and Observe Dynamic Updates
tasks.goal: Inject a ConfigMap via volume mount to access its data as files, then update the ConfigMap and observe that the files inside the container are refreshed automatically. Experience the difference from the environment variable approach firsthand.
tasks.prompt:
1. Write a Pod manifest (volume-pod.yaml) that mounts app-config as a volume.
   - Image: busybox:1.36
   - Command: sh -c 'sleep 3600'
   - Mount app-config at /etc/app/config as readOnly.
2. After creating the Pod, use kubectl exec to list the files under /etc/app/config/ and read the contents of the LOG_LEVEL file.
3. Use kubectl patch or kubectl edit to change LOG_LEVEL in app-config to debug.
4. Wait approximately 30–60 seconds, then use kubectl exec to confirm that /etc/app/config/LOG_LEVEL now contains debug.
5. Based on your observations, write one sentence describing the difference in update behavior between environment variable injection and volume mount injection.
tasks.starter:
# volume-pod.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: volume-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ["sh", "-c", "sleep 3600"]
      volumeMounts:
        - name: ____          # must match the name in volumes
          mountPath: ____
          readOnly: true
  volumes:
    - name: ____
      configMap:
        name: ____
tasks.solution:
# volume-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: volume-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ["sh", "-c", "sleep 3600"]
      volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/app/config
          readOnly: true
  volumes:
    - name: config-volume
      configMap:
        name: app-config

# Create the Pod
kubectl apply -f volume-pod.yaml
kubectl get pod volume-pod

# List files and check the initial value
kubectl exec volume-pod -- ls /etc/app/config
# Expected output: APP_REGION  LOG_LEVEL
kubectl exec volume-pod -- cat /etc/app/config/LOG_LEVEL
# Output: info

# Update the ConfigMap value (patch method)
kubectl patch configmap app-config \
  --type merge \
  -p '{"data":{"LOG_LEVEL":"debug"}}'

# Wait ~30-60 seconds for the automatic refresh
# (kubelet sync interval defaults to 60 seconds)
kubectl exec volume-pod -- cat /etc/app/config/LOG_LEVEL
# Output: debug  (update confirmed)

# [Note] With environment variable injection, you must restart the Pod to see the change:
# kubectl delete pod envfrom-pod && kubectl apply -f envfrom-pod.yaml
tasks.explanation: When a ConfigMap is mounted as a volume, each key is created as a separate file under mountPath. In this exercise, two files are created: /etc/app/config/LOG_LEVEL and /etc/app/config/APP_REGION, each containing the corresponding value. When you update the ConfigMap with kubectl patch, the kubelet refreshes the volume files automatically on its next sync cycle (default: 60 seconds). By contrast, values injected via environment variables (envFrom or valueFrom) are fixed at Pod creation time — updating the ConfigMap has no effect until the Pod is restarted. This is why applications that require hot-reloading of config (e.g., nginx config files, feature flags) are better served by the volume mount approach.
hints: volumeMounts.name and volumes.name must be exactly the same string. Changing only one side will put the Pod into an Error state.
Verify that the output of kubectl exec <pod> -- ls /etc/app/config shows key names as filenames. If no files appear, check that volumes.configMap.name matches the actual ConfigMap name.

It is normal for the update not to happen immediately. Wait for the kubelet sync cycle (default 60 seconds), or in a minikube environment, run kubectl exec repeatedly to watch for the change. If the value has not changed after more than 30 seconds, confirm the patch was actually applied by running kubectl describe configmap app-config.
